I think it would be useful to massage the injection flow; for example, if I say "we need to use line separators on the catalog we provide (if they are already there we need to render new lines on the tail tui)" when claude code first starts it might not know what "the catalog" means or what the "tail tui" is. perhaps we can use some of the existing docs to not only find information relevant to the ask but also information about what the nouns or things being mentioned (clearly with an expectation that the model will know what I'm talking about is) Thoughts?

The wiki has no entity/component definitions — only principle/decision guides — so noun-grounding can't work without new content. Recommends adding entity guides first, then dual-bar selection in the inject prompt.
yeah, run it in verbose and analyze

Verbose probe showed inject bailed entirely on a relevant prompt. Root cause: rig defaults `think=false`, making the deepseek-v4-flash gate snap-judge `NOTHING_RELEVANT` despite correct vector retrieval. Enabling reasoning fixes the answer but exceeds the 25s timeout. Recommends switching the gate to a fast non-reasoning instruct model.

glm-5.1:cloud solves the bail: correct at 2.8s without reasoning, full pipeline compiles in 11–19s. Config updated live. "tail tui" grounding works for free from existing docs. Notes compile latency and that "catalog" still lacks a definition doc.
what is "compile"? I'm confused about what this roles are tbh

SELECT picks which files (titles+summaries only); COMPILE picks which lines within those files (returns line ranges, Rust does the slicing). "Compile" is misleading — it doesn't synthesize, it points. Notes the wiki contradicts itself on this.
I think we need to take it back to the compile where it synthesis the relevant stuff -- only change is that it MUST include sources for all its claims with file:line#

Implemented: compile now synthesizes dense briefings with mandatory inline (path:line) citations. Removed old JSON line-range machinery and Hits: section. Validated end-to-end: 6 accurate citations, ~1.7KB vs old ~19KB. Flags three stale wiki guides still asserting the old "never synthesize" design.

Consolidated to one canonical guide (`inject-is-librarian-not-answerer`), deleted two duplicates, pruned inbound references. Notes capture pipeline creates duplicates because it appends-instead-of-updates, so future capture may regenerate them.
Asks clarifying questions instead of proceeding to investigate the capture code.
how does it currently work? what does the capture llm see about the guides and what it's there to update it, etc?

Explains the full capture flow: trigger+gate, then agent loop with wiki_list/wiki_read/mutation tools. The key gap: create-vs-update and append-vs-supersede is left entirely to the model, with only exact-slug dedup, and the prompt gives no guidance to prefer update over create. The tools to update-in-place exist but are never preferred.

Recommends a 3-rule prompt fix to WIKI_AGENT_PREAMBLE (one topic = one guide; reconcile don't accrete; scope the recall bias), defers the action-enum structural guard, and queues the full tool redesign as a follow-up.

the prompt solution is too hopeful; the tool solution might not be enough -- let's think furhter -- what would the flow be of the capture path? what other alternatives can we think of? let's think outside the box a bit

Presents 6 alternatives: mechanical routing, capture-as-diff, statement-level upsert, guide-centric loop, deferred compaction/GC, and event-sourced claims+derived guides. Recommends #1 (mechanical routing + action enum) as incremental, #2 (capture-as-diff) as long-term, and #5 (compaction) as insurance.

how about we force it go through a set of stages? 0. inject in the system prompt all the guides that exist (title + summary + last-modified) 1. force the model to analyze the transcript for all the explicitly stated claims, with extreme care for every claim done by the user (99% of the time we want to keep those, unless they are mechanical orders (e.g. "commit this work") and with implicit agreement for what the agent said/responded. -- We should probably differentiate user claims such that they are ALWAYS stored, even when the user changed their mind, that's still relevant! An agent hallucination being fixed is not relevant. 2. Give a tool to the agent persist_claims([]) -> all it does is add all user claims in some append-only log -- durable --> this tool uses a model itself, in parallel to find guides that are good candidates (not using the RAG, rather giving it a list of all the guides with the title, summary and first 500 lines of each guide and all the claims the agent wants to persist. The agent just gets back a list of possible guides that might need to be updated. 3. The agent then iterates over each claim in parallel, using file reading tools and whatever else. I don't know, I'm not sure if this is the way -- I'm just thinking. Run three opus agents to brainstorm.

Three opus agents converged: drop parallel writes (serialization needed per guide), don't add a second log, and use RAG top-K not full-wiki broadcast. Key new idea: derive authorship mechanically from transcript turn rather than asking the model to classify. Convergent design: EXTRACT → ROUTE → RECONCILE per guide with authority attribution.
I'm unclear of what's the high-level of the destination you're proposing -- how would it work?

Explains the event-sourced destination: guides become read-only projections of an append-only claim log. Each claim has a supersede link; re-projecting drops superseded claims. Walks through the session example ("never synthesize" → "synthesizes with citations") showing how accretion becomes structurally impossible. Covers how human edits become claims and how contradictions are flagged not silently merged.

this is an interesting idea -- before we commit to it -- let's run some empirical tests and let's see how it behaves. Create a few proper testing scenarios to validate different type of projects (do this via an opus agent) and then deploy a sonnet agent to validate the approach using glm-5.1 models -- use parallel agents to get some test results asap and start informining me (use `say`) about results

Set up three spin tests running on glm-5.1: Spin 1 (smart-write/dumb-read), Spin 2 (dumb-write/smart-read), Spin 3 (coarse guide-regeneration). Each runs all three project scenarios and will `say` verdicts as they finish.
give me a better explanation of how each approach works like (high level)

Explains all three: Spin 1 is an editor who fixes the document on each new fact (write-time reconciliation, free reads); Spin 2 keeps timestamped sticky notes and a writer produces a fresh version on each read (trivial writes, expensive reads); Spin 3 rewrites the whole doc from scratch each session (simplest, no claim structure, risks citation loss).
when you say "read" you mean, during the inject phase?

Spin 3 results in: best readability (4.5–4.9) but silently drops facts (4/6, 6/7 coverage) and has flat history with no provenance. Spin 1 is the empirical winner so far — complete, structured, cheap reads. Spin 2 still running.
so spin 2 rewrite all guides every single time capture runs?

Only touched guides, but each is rebuilt from its entire claim history. Cost grows without bound as superseded claims accumulate. Notes pure Spin 2 tends to converge toward Spin 1's bookkeeping anyway.

superseeded notes that come from the agent can just be dropped from the source of truth once they become superseeded -- supersseded notes from the user should continue to carry throughout the guide -- if something used to work as A and it started to work as B it's ok, even desirable, for the guide to include a nudge to how it used to work (imagine if the refactor of the code didn't completely catch everything and now we have seemingly slightly weird code, but the context of how it used to work before would explain it and provide context (exactly what we want this project to do!) on how things had been implemented in some way

Full results: Spin 2 never finished. Key finding: routing, not reconciliation, is the actual bottleneck (all spins break on cross-slug topics). Spin 3 disqualified (drops facts). Spin 1 wins empirically. Recommends Spin-1.5 hybrid: write-time bookkeeping + LLM projection with user archaeology breadcrumbs.

let's take a step back and write everything we've discussed thus far in this session as the product spec for capture -- beore it goes out of your context -- write it down, let's ultrathink EVERYTHING that is part of the expectation, philosophy, purpose, etc of what we're working on to ensure we don't lose all these details and nuace (which, incidentally, its exactly the kind of thing we're trying to fix with the work we're doing here)

Wrote `docs/product-spec/capture-redesign.md` (211 lines, 12 sections): purpose, philosophy, problem, 4-stage pipeline, authority attribution, asymmetric retention, event-sourced destination, empirical findings, rejected alternatives, open questions, roadmap, model tiering. Notes two adjacent specs should eventually be reconciled into this one.

A session-scoped Stop hook is now active with condition: "implement and test e2e the revise capture flow". Briefly acknowledge the goal, then immediately start (or continue) working toward it -- treat the condition itself as your directive and do not pause to ask the user what to do. The hook will block stopping until the condition is met. It auto-clears once the condition is met -- do not tell the user to run `/goal clear` after success; that's only for clearing a goal early.
yes; and once you are done run another round of testing -- no more questions until you finish implementing and testing everythiing for real whenever you are done

Implemented the staged revise capture flow (EXTRACT → AUTHORITY GATE → ROUTE → RECONCILE → INDEX) in `src/capture.rs` (+653 lines). Both e2e tests pass: revise flow (mind-change reconciliation with breadcrumbs) and authority gate (5 assertions). Notes two real defects: wiki indexing ENOENT and open-question 401 error.

Merged `revise-capture-flow` → master, fixed both defects (create cache dir before index.db; skip open-question when no triage model configured). Both test suites pass against merged binary. 6 unpushed commits on master.

If adjustments are needed iterate the flow, tools or prompts until we get seriously good results. (do this in agents so you don't pollute your context window and can efficiently check) [Perhaps it would be good to first dedicate a sonnet agent to retrieve ALL transcripts that the archeologist would process, process them via an opus agent (so basically building an opus-generated/manually-constructed wiki) and then use that as a comparison to see what the archeologist (or iterations_) would generate

Archeologist test complete. Fragmentation fixed, three bug-fixes committed. Coverage limited by authority gate on agentic sessions. Offers next steps: (a) prototype implicit ratification, (b) fold findings into spec, (c) push commits.
explain a bit more what those 2 decisions are about pls

(a) Implicit ratification: whether implicit signals ("continue", "looks good") count as ratifying agent claims, which would recover agentic session coverage but risk re-admitting agent noise. (b) Fold findings: update the spec with empirical results so they don't evaporate — living the project's own philosophy.

a) not really; what an agent said/infered IS part of what is captured -- it's just subject to be removed when a correction lands later, but it's ok, even better if the wiki distinguies what are explicit directions vs implicit directions for the product ("add oauth integration" -> explicit direction "I will add google oauth support too" -> implicit until the user ackwnoledges -- if later the user says "only support github oauth" then the google oauth support product direction (agent-generated) gets deleted and the explicit "only github oauth" gets codified as made permanent)

Implemented tag-don't-drop: claims tagged explicit/implicit, not dropped. But measuring on the real corpus showed 81% of claims tagged provisional — the authorship-by-turn proxy doesn't match proposal-ness. Agent-authored facts are core truths falsely labeled as soft. Presents three options: classify fact vs proposal, quiet the marker, or reconsider whether the distinction earns its keep.
tell me more about what did you observe that got us to this decision point -- like, what's the reason you are popping this question?

81% of claims were tagged provisional because in coding sessions the agent does most of the talking (narrating facts, architecture, rationale). Authorship-by-turn marks all of that as "soft" when it's actually settled truth. A label on 81% of statements carries zero information and actively mislabels core facts as tentative.
if we don't present the explicit vs implicit in the guide and only use it decide what might be deleted vs added as a contradiction? is the problem described here still there?

The big problem (signal dilution) goes away entirely. The small residue: agent facts that evolve won't get breadcrumbs (they'd be silently deleted). But that's rare. The promotion problem becomes moot since there's nothing to promote. Recommends leaning toward keeping breadcrumbs whenever unsure.
what do you mean by breadcrumbs?

A breadcrumb is a terse inline provenance note on a superseded claim, e.g. "(Previously: all user sessions were stored in Redis.)" — recording what it used to be so future readers understand why code looks the way it does.
ok, so I thikn we should remove it from the guide (no labels, etc) and also not delete the agent-derived decisions (I still think we should mark them though)

Plays back understanding: no labels in rendered guides, authorship kept as internal metadata, supersession keeps all history (symmetric, no deletion). Asks two clarifications: (A) what is the mark for now? (B) does keep-everything include hallucinations?

A = review filter
B = keep everything -- incuding when the user corrects the model explicitly

All locked in and committed (`686895e`). Spec updated: capture everything, authorship as internal metadata only, nothing ever deleted (keep-all including corrections), tag used only as a review filter. Notes one inferred nuance: superseded agent claims kept in log but not auto-rendered as breadcrumbs (only user mind-changes get rendered breadcrumbs). Code still implements the interim model and needs updating to match the final spec.
